foundationATV.title: Who Are We?
foundationATV.subtitle: We are a non-profit organization that
foundationATV.description: Seeks to reduce student dropout rates in Colombia, improve retention and graduation rates, this being our way of contributing to achieving a country with more education and consequently more development.

change_theory.title: THEORY OF CHANGE
change_theory.description: Identify students with vulnerabilities and provide comprehensive support through an ecosystem of strategies focused on academic, economic and socio-emotional aspects for retention.

values.title: VALUES
values.description: At the Antivirus Foundation for Dropout Prevention, we do our work collaboratively, with passion, integrity, autonomy, empathy, commitment and camaraderie. We are supportive and innovative and have a great vocation for service.

mission.title: MISSION
mission.description: Increase graduation rates of students in technology careers and programs, through strategies to promote retention and prevent dropout.

target_audience.title: TARGET AUDIENCE
target_audience.description: We work with students from IT careers and programs in Secondary and Post-secondary Education. We focus our intervention on students vulnerable to dropout.

vision.title: VISION
vision.description: In 5 years we will have a national presence, in the 10 best universities in the country, where we will have reduced the dropout rate by 10 percentage points (from 50 to 40%). Additionally, we will be replicating a reference model, whose success can be measured qualitatively and quantitatively.

items.consolidate_model: Consolidate a model with processes for identifying vulnerabilities, intervention and result indicators.
items.explore_interventions: Explore intervention models from schools to contribute to reducing dropout in higher education.
items.involve_actors: Know and involve other actors in retention work (Rectors, Deans, MEN, Companies, among others).
items.reduce_dropout: Help reduce dropout in other universities and programs to refine our model and generate income that contributes to the sustainability of the Antivirus Foundation.

purpose.title: PURPOSE
purpose.description: Build a prosperous, peaceful, harmonious, technological and competitive country, in which all young people have access to education and are good human beings, with goals and empowered in their lives. Thanks to this, we will overcome poverty and become a reference for development.

banner.alt: Close-up of a hand with the institutional wristband over a group of young students, symbolizing a commitment to education.

reconocidos-por-la-facultad-de-minas.alt: Recognized by the Faculty of Mines on its 139th anniversary
reconocidos-por-la-facultad-de-minas.title: RECOGNIZED BY THE FACULTY OF MINES ON ITS 139TH ANNIVERSARY

detail.title: RECOGNIZED BY THE FACULTY OF MINES ON ITS 139TH ANNIVERSARY
content: A few years ago, we dreamed of more young people being able to stay in university. Today, that dream allows us to experience moments like this.

<bold>The Faculty of Mines of the National University of Colombia, Medellín campus,</bold> as part of the celebration of its <bold>139th anniversary,</bold> recognized us as <bold>strategic partners for our work in favor of student retention.</bold>

The recognition bears the name of the FATV, but it truly belongs to every student who decides not to give up, to every conversation held during difficult times, to every partner who believes, and to every person who is part of the journey.
<bold>Thank you to the Faculty of Mines for believing in the power of partnerships that transform lives and for allowing us to walk hand in hand in this purpose.</bold>
We are very proud to be recognized by the Faculty of Mines on its 139th anniversary. This synergy represents teamwork and the commitment to ensure that no young person has to abandon their studies.

detail.title: UNIVERSITY DROPOUT
content: I have a vision for my country: a prosperous, peaceful, harmonious, technological and competitive place, where everyone has access to education and is a good human being with purpose and empowerment. To achieve this, I focus on addressing the shortage of talent in software development and the high university dropout rate, particularly at the Faculty of Engineering of the University of Antioquia.

The private sector has experienced a deficit of software engineers and programmers, while universities do not meet the demand. We currently graduate only around 7,000 engineers/programmers per year, which leaves us with a talent deficit of between 90,000 and 240,000 professionals. This moves us away from development and the knowledge economy.

University dropout, especially in Systems Engineering, is a national problem. The University of Antioquia faces a high dropout rate, which has a devastating social cost. I propose a mentoring program where advanced students help first-semester students, which has been shown to reduce dropout by 20%. In addition, a system can be established in which companies financially support students in exchange for a percentage of their salaries once they graduate.

It is also important to address students' economic difficulties and family pressure. Here, the private sector can contribute through computer donations, support in structuring financing instruments and the participation of their collaborators in volunteer work.

In short, a joint approach is needed to achieve a shared vision of development in the country. With mentoring programs, financial support from companies and the active participation of the private sector, we can reduce the shortage of talent and university dropout, paving the way towards a prosperous and technological future.

importancia-de-los-datos.alt: The importance of data to end dropout
importancia-de-los-datos.title: THE IMPORTANCE OF DATA TO END DROPOUT

detail.title: THE IMPORTANCE OF DATA TO END DROPOUT
content: Data can help enrich stories and can provide valuable information about the things we live every day, providing a source of practical wisdom and giving us guidance on human affairs. We know that talking about algorithms to deal with human lives may seem like a juxtaposition, since for many people the word algorithm evokes arcane and registrable machinery of large companies. But, an algorithm is simply a finite sequence of steps that are used to solve a problem, in which the data provides valuable information to forge each step taken.

There is a recent idea called: “human algorithm design”. This seeks to find better solutions to the challenges that people face every day, whether to track climate change in the world, streamline the search results for an apartment or to end university dropout. While we will delve into the latter in more detail, because this is our purpose, we want to first show how even the most mundane problems can be solved with data. Thus, let's think about the case where Pepita wants to buy an apartment. When this situation was presented to her, Pepita intuited that she required a balance between looking at apartments and choosing the one she wanted. In this way, she foresaw that she should look for enough apartments to establish a standard and, then, commit to the first apartment she sees that exceeds her standard. This means that you need 37% of the time you have to be able to set goals. This type of problem belongs to the class of problems called optimal stopping problems.

The previous example is just an illustration of the capacity of data and statistics. Life is full of problems that are, simply, very difficult. An example of this is the high dropout rate we have in Colombia (50% on average in any faculty and 70% in engineering faculties). This terrifying figure says more about the intrinsic difficulties of this problem than about the fallibility of the human brain. To solve this problem we need to understand its fundamental structure and for this we need data. However, the key value of data is the ability to make the most of it: find relevant associations within the data, manipulate it into manageable sets and then create applications to better represent what is discovered. This was learned by the “Graduation Rate Champion” of the University of Texas, when he was given the challenge of increasing the graduation rate from 51% in 2021 to 70% in 2016.

To achieve his goal, the Graduation Rate Champion recruited a team of data scientists in the university's institutional research office, who were specialized in predictive analytics. By deconstructing the records of tens of thousands of recent students at this University, they were able to develop a model statistic that combined fourteen separate variables. From family income to SAT score, college class rank and parents' educational history, in a single algorithm that could reliably predict the probability that an admitted student would graduate. This team called this new tool the Four-Year Graduation Index Dashboard. (Taugh, 2019) The gain that increasing the number of graduates implies for a university is reflected in a very high social mobility rate. The passage through a university forges the identity formation process of adolescents and really has a positive impact on the way people's lives turn out; how happy they will be and how well they will negotiate the challenges of work, family, friendship and community.

una-oportunidad-que-cruzo-fronteras.alt: An opportunity that crossed borders for students
una-oportunidad-que-cruzo-fronteras.title: AN OPPORTUNITY THAT CROSSED BORDERS

detail.title: AN OPPORTUNITY THAT CROSSED BORDERS
content: An opportunity that crossed borders, thanks to the collaboration of <bold>Fundación Gente Unida,</bold> <bold>Fundación Sofía Pérez de Soto,</bold> <bold>Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción, and their families.</bold> 🇧🇷🇨🇴💻

Three students, one shared dream! <bold>Libardo Navarro,</bold> <bold>Mateo Álvarez,</bold> and <bold>Efraín Gómez</bold> took the talent of the <bold>Universidad Nacional,</bold> Medellín campus, to the <bold>Brazilian ICPC Summer School 2026,</bold> one of the most demanding competitive programming spaces in Latin America.

For days, they faced advanced algorithms, new data structures, and the level of the best teams on the continent. But beyond the code, they experienced something that cannot be learned in a classroom: community, discipline, and a global vision. 🌎

As the Antivirus Foundation for Dropout Prevention, we believe in talent that perseveres. That is why we supported their participation in this international event, convinced that supporting also means opening doors.

Today, they return better prepared for their next challenge: the Super Regional in March, where they will seek a ticket to the World Finals. And we remain here, backing their dreams until they become achievements.
If you also believe in the power of education and support, walk with us.

vincular-para-transformar.alt: Article: Connecting to transform: a communicative perspective on education
vincular-para-transformar.title: CONNECTING TO TRANSFORM: A COMMUNICATIVE PERSPECTIVE ON EDUCATION

detail.title: CONNECTING TO TRANSFORM: A COMMUNICATIVE PERSPECTIVE ON EDUCATION
content: Communicating in the educational and social sphere is not just about transmitting information: above all, it is an act of responsibility toward the future of those who listen to us.

<bold>How can communication become a bridge between vocational guidance and the future that public school students dream of?</bold> There are projects that are lived simply through the role for which we were hired, and then there are others that end up transforming how we understand that role itself. Being part of a socio-vocational guidance project aimed at 10th and 11th-grade students in public schools has been, for me, the latter: an experience as challenging as it is enriching, especially considering the role communication plays in a process that, on the surface, might seem purely pedagogical.

I have always believed that <bold>education is the foundation of societal transformation.</bold> That is why the opportunity to plant seeds in these young people—seeds that will eventually bear fruit, allowing them to pursue the lives they desire, project themselves with sound judgment, and contribute to the country around them—is so special. But every seed needs more than just fertile soil to germinate: it needs constant watering. In a project like this, that watering consists, in large part, of: <bold>the way an opportunity is presented, the language used to explain a life decision, and the closeness with which we support someone who is still not quite sure what they want to be and do.</bold>

Currently, awakening genuine interest through language and audiovisual content is subject to numerous obstacles. Overstimulation and information overload continue to reduce people's attention spans, and young people are one of the populations most affected by this.

Added to this are the complex contextual conditions faced by many public school students, which implies the need for a differentiated approach: <bold>communication that cannot be generic or standardized, but rather capable of recognizing those particular realities and speaking to young people from where they truly are.</bold>

That has been my banner and goal since taking on this role as an audiovisual communicator at the Fundación Antivirus: to seek and develop strategies so that the social component of this project is fully supported by our communicative efforts, allowing us to connect with young people in an authentic, close, and comprehensive way. I say "connect" and not just "inform" because that is the central difference between functional communication and transformative communication. You can inform someone about technical programs, universities, or scholarships without any student feeling that the message belongs to them. <bold>But when you succeed in building a genuine connection, information ceases to be an external fact and becomes a real possibility for their own lives.</bold>

Communicating in the educational and social sphere is not just about transmitting information: above all, it is an act of responsibility toward the future of those who listen to us. If education and socio-vocational guidance plant the seeds of change, the purpose of communication is to help them find the light they need to grow.

la-crisis-en-educacion-superior.alt: Antivirus Foundation collaborator and students in their respective educational settings.
la-crisis-en-educacion-superior.title: ON THE CRISIS IN HIGHER EDUCATION

detail.title: ON THE CRISIS IN HIGHER EDUCATION
content: Today, there is a multidimensional crisis in higher education in Colombia, characterized by funding issues, decreasing access to advanced studies, an exponential rise in dropout rates, and low graduation rates nationwide. This situation calls us to rethink, organize, and implement contextualized support processes that promote quality opportunities, informed professional choices, and comprehensive retention in the country's technical, technological, and university programs.

This crisis is reflected in data that highlights the massive scale of the problem regarding access: according to the 'Observatorio de la Universidad Colombiana' (2025), in various articles published between 2019 and 2025, there has been a significant downward trend since the pandemic, with a 8% to 10% decrease in enrollment in both public and private universities compared to previous decades. This becomes even more relevant when considering figures from the 14th edition of the Deloitte Gen Z and Millennial Survey (2025), which found that '43% of young people in Colombia decide not to enter university.' However, the problem goes beyond access; according to CEIPA (2025), dropout rates in Colombia stand at '46% in university programs and exceed 50% in technical and technological programs, being especially severe in low socioeconomic strata (up to 70%).'

Faced with this crisis, there are many value judgments, conjectures, and accusations about who or what caused it. In everyday conversations about the problem, it is not rare to hear on the street or read on social media adults claiming: 'Today’s kids don't want to study out of pure laziness,' 'There are so many opportunities, and young people don't take advantage of them,' 'They don't want to study because they spend all day glued to TikTok thinking they will become influencers,' or 'The snowflake generation can't handle studying and working.' These comments label current generations as the culprits of the educational problem. But is this exclusively a problem of a lack of interest among young people?

In my experience, when you go to schools and talk to adolescent students, you frequently encounter difficulties, doubts, and fears: 'I'm not sure what to study,' 'I don't know many universities,' 'I'm afraid of studying for 5 years and ending up earning minimum wage,' 'I feel guilty about not being able to financially support my family while studying.' When you talk to teachers, they often comment that their students 'Prefer to start a business rather than pursue a degree,' 'Are more interested in the possibility of generating income online,' 'Think more about the possibility of seeking opportunities in other countries,' or 'Prefer the idea of learning techniques and skills in a short time to enter the job market quickly.'

On the other hand, within the university sphere, we can understand the phenomenon in other dimensions. When you support university students who are at risk of dropping out, or who have finally decided to pause or leave their studies, you find in their life stories all kinds of motives that go beyond their individual will: 'I have to support my family financially,' 'I have to choose between the bus fare to get to university or having enough to eat,' 'I have severe anxiety and no health insurance or means to pay for treatment,' 'I chose my major because it was what my family wanted,' 'I feel I have academic gaps from high school, and now I don't know how to study properly for this subject,' 'I come from the countryside; it's very hard for me to adapt to the city and be away from my family,' 'I'm afraid of not finding a good professional future that justifies all the effort and money invested in this degree.'

Therefore, the experiences in schools and universities help us understand that part of the root of the problem goes beyond a supposed general lack of interest from young people and <bold>corresponds more to socioeconomic subsistence needs in a high-cost-of-living context, fears about the future in an increasingly unstable labor market, lack of awareness regarding educational opportunities, mental health issues, and learning problems inherited from a deteriorated traditional education system, in addition to new interests in fast and flexible training.</bold> If we add to all this the funding problems universities face and the decline in the birth rate, which results in fewer young people of school age, the outlook for higher education becomes critical.

Consequently, it is worth remembering Zygmunt Bauman (2000), who in his work 'Liquid Modernity' describes how we transitioned from a solid modernity (19th Century), characterized by stable institutions and conditions (long-term jobs, lasting relationships, better conditions for housing access and social services, clear and lasting norms, traditional family nuclei, etc.), to a liquid modernity (21st Century) with unstable characteristics (temporary jobs, ephemeral relationships, privatization and high cost of living, changing norms, fragmented and distant families, etc.). Thus, one of the quintessential children of solid modernity was the university, which was born to train future professionals under conditions of economic stability, solid family support, and preparation for a stable labor market. However, in the midst of liquid modernity, the university faces problems of access, retention, and dropout anchored to an academic structure and a training model coming from the past, resorting to old solutions for new problems.

Does this mean the solution is to give up on the university and professional training as an educational project for democratization and progress? Not necessarily. Since every crisis is also an opportunity for change and improvement, this situation can enable and foster a deep review by the State, academia, and social organizations on how to understand the problem in a contextualized way to promote transformations in access, training, and retention in higher education according to the new conditions of this era.

In that vein, one of the main tools and strategies to foster a necessary transformation in the midst of an educational crisis is everything related to socio-pedagogical support from the final years of high school through the subsequent years of university training. It promotes informed and conscious vocational choices among students, personalized academic support processes in critical subjects, spaces for listening and emotional containment in moments of crisis, guidance and inter-institutional coordination for solutions promoted by universities and other public-private actors, as well as first-hand data and experiences on the current situation of dropout in the educational environment, which can provide vital information for decision-making and the necessary changes to ensure higher quality in access, retention, and graduation in higher education.

. . . In this regard, the Antivirus Foundation for Dropout Prevention has a vast wealth of experience and established potential to contribute to mitigating the crisis, but that is a subject to be expanded upon in another article!

acompanamiento-becarios-rurales.alt: The importance of data in ending dropout rates
acompanamiento-becarios-rurales.title: COMPREHENSIVE SUPPORT FOR RURAL SCHOLARS IN COLOMBIA

detail.title: COMPREHENSIVE SUPPORT FOR RURAL SCHOLARS IN COLOMBIA
content: Relocating to urban environments, cultural adaptation, academic challenges, and the distance from their support networks create a scenario of vulnerability that can affect their well-being and persistence in higher education.

Access to higher education in Colombia in 2026 continues to be marked by structural inequalities. Students from rural backgrounds aiming to enter Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) <bold>face not only the cultural shifts required by moving away from their territories but also systematic gaps that transcend economic factors, impacting both their academic performance and emotional well-being.</bold> In this context, scholarship programs with a rural focus represent an opportunity for social mobility; however, their impact largely depends on the existence of support strategies that recognize the particularities of this population.

Rural scholarship recipients <bold>often transition from schools with limited resources to rigorous university environments. This transition involves a shift in learning dynamics,</bold> especially in foundational areas of knowledge, where accumulated gaps become evident. In the medium term, these gaps can turn into unattainable goals, leading to frustration and, eventually, a risk of dropping out.

<bold>Relocating to urban environments, cultural adaptation, academic challenges, and the distance from their support networks,</bold> create a scenario of vulnerability that can affect their well-being and persistence in higher education. Therefore, at the Antivirus Foundation for Dropout Prevention, we are committed to interdisciplinary support and personalized, continuous follow-up.

We recognize that <bold>comprehensive, contextualized support</bold> that addresses both emotional and academic aspects is a priority approach to mitigating these gaps. Academic support allows for the strengthening of basic competencies, particularly in critical areas, through personalized peer-to-peer tutoring provided by students who have already navigated the university experience. We focus on building support networks within their current environment, while simultaneously offering an emotional component that provides tools for managing the challenges inherent in this university stage.

<bold>Comprehensive support not only promotes student persistence but also contributes to the development of their life projects. The training of rural scholars and their potential return to their territories benefits local development.</bold> Therefore, strengthening support programs for scholarship recipients should not be considered a supplement, but a structural component of education. Recognizing the inequalities of origin and acting upon them through contextualized interventions is a fundamental step toward equal opportunity in higher education in Colombia. Investing in support for rural students is an investment in the future of the country's territories.

el-docente-hoy.alt: Antivirus Foundation collaborator and students in their respective educational settings.
el-docente-hoy.title: THE ROLE OF TEACHERS IN TODAY'S EDUCATION

detail.title: THE ROLE OF TEACHERS IN TODAY'S EDUCATION
content: Education and learning processes are not exclusive to school; we learn on the streets, in art and sports groups, with friends, at home, and in every space we inhabit. Because every gaze, every gesture, and every word teaches.

Therefore, when we want to discover who we are, we must delve into those lessons learned in childhood—things we know, though we have no idea how we came to know them. <bold>While education is not exclusive to schools or universities, these institutions have become spaces for 'conscious' learning.</bold> I can affirm that, regardless of where learning takes place, it is always through our relationship with others that we build ourselves; even the absence of another teaches us, but there is always 'an other'.

Now, who is this 'other' who explicitly assumes the role of teaching? Perhaps you have called them a tutor, a master, or a professor—each term carries conceptual variations that change depending on whom you ask. Whoever takes on this responsibility assumes a second role: that of an interpreter of reality. Within a formal space, they connect with others to share the 'knowledge' necessary to understand and inhabit social life. <bold>This implies that education must respond to the conditions and needs of its context, and it is the teacher's job to make that context more digestible.</bold> Historically, monarchies taught politics and religion, while the industrial era focused on machinery and production because those were the requirements of the time. If we start from that notion, then what is being taught today? Is today's teacher acting as an interpreter of current reality? Or are they still reproducing the same models from a century ago, simply under modern names?

<bold>De Zubiría (2013) stated: 'Young people who will live in the 21st century are being educated by teachers from the 20th century, but with pedagogical models and curricula from the 19th century.' (p. 3).</bold>

Current reality is full of influencers, technology, pressure on how one should live, mindfulness, and many other new ways of existence that tell us there is always more and it is never enough—often involving unattainable and idealistic goals. <bold>We live in a society that talks more and more about the 'self,' yet lacks grounded tools to achieve it (or conversely, offers so many that one doesn't know where to start).</bold> Many teachers—I cannot claim all, but it is evident that a great number do—perform a 'translation' of reality. And like any translation, it omits truths, ignores feelings and implicit messages, and focuses solely on content. We used to value others based on what they did; now, we value them based on what they know. Paradoxically, the 'being' is undervalued, even though it is what reality demands most. <bold>Right there, in that paradox and contradiction, lies the place of the teacher: not as a mere translator of realities, but as an interpreter who accompanies human self-construction through being, knowing, and doing.</bold>

retos-comunicativos.alt: Article on the communicative challenges of the FATV for student persistence
retos-comunicativos.title: COMMUNICATIVE CHALLENGES OF THE FATV FOR STUDENT PERSISTENCE

detail.title: COMMUNICATIVE CHALLENGES OF THE FATV FOR STUDENT PERSISTENCE
content: Communication at the FATV must be strategic, personalized, and centered on creating high-value content. Overcoming information overload and tailoring our messages to the real needs of our audiences is essential to driving a positive impact, fostering educational persistence, and, ultimately, contributing to social transformation.

Communication plays a vital role in any social endeavor, especially at the Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción (FATV), where we strive to contribute positively to education. Educational quality depends not only on material resources but also on how key stakeholders—students, teachers, families, and society at large—connect, interact, and engage.

One of the core challenges we face as a foundation is effectively engaging academic communities and students to promote persistence in higher education. In this context, communication is not just about sharing information; it is about building a bridge of trust, motivation, and ongoing support that empowers young people to overcome adversity and complete their degrees.

First, regarding our communication with students, the challenge is the <bold>information overload characteristic of the digital age,</bold> where young people are constantly bombarded with diverse messages—ranging from advertising and institutional alerts to support campaigns and government programs. This excess can lead to confusion, apathy, and even the loss of relevant information.

To overcome this, we focus on producing <bold>quality and relevant content.</bold> Creating value means providing useful, understandable, and motivating information that truly addresses students' needs. Our content must be <bold>personalized and relatable,</bold> tailored to the socioeconomic, academic, or socio-emotional realities of our students. This also requires a shift toward <bold>bidirectional communication,</bold> where students do not just receive information, but have a dedicated space to voice their concerns, needs, and aspirations. <bold>Active listening and feedback are fundamental to building a support bond that makes students feel understood and empowered.</bold>

A second challenge lies in reaching academic communities and social organizations that align with our mission. To address this, we have developed a strategy to increase the visibility of our work, achievements, metrics, and impactful testimonials. This allows us to connect with more organizations, create further intervention opportunities, and, in turn, support more students.

Finally, on a broader level, the challenge is the <bold>fragmentation of communication channels.</bold> To reach our audiences effectively, we must manage these channels coherently, ensuring our message is delivered successfully and does not get lost in the noise.

In conclusion, for 2025, <bold>communication at the FATV must be strategic, personalized, and centered on creating high-value content. Overcoming information overload and tailoring our messages to the real needs of our audiences is essential to driving a positive impact, fostering educational persistence, and, ultimately, contributing to social transformation.</bold>

permanencia-educativa-de-calidad.alt: The importance of data to end dropout rates
permanencia-educativa-de-calidad.title: QUALITY EDUCATIONAL PERSISTENCE

detail.title: QUALITY EDUCATIONAL PERSISTENCE
content: Educational persistence refers to the continuity of students within the educational system. It ensures that students not only remain enrolled but do so under optimal learning conditions.

It is not enough for them to simply attend class; it is fundamental that they receive a meaningful, inclusive, and equitable education that allows them to develop skills and knowledge effectively while achieving socio-emotional well-being. In a constantly changing world, university well-being must be dynamic and person-centered. This concept involves several key factors and the planning of concrete objectives:

<bold>Access and equity:</bold> ensuring that all students can persist and receive a quality education. Implementing anti-discrimination policies for any reason, while activating technologies and support options that ensure full participation in university life. <bold>Adequate conditions:</bold> offering appropriate infrastructure, updated teaching materials, trained faculty, and a safe and motivating environment.

<bold>Relevant and flexible curriculum:</bold> designing a curriculum that responds to the needs of today's world—dynamic and adjustable—that fosters critical thinking, creativity, and problem-solving. <bold>Psychosocial support:</bold> implementing strategies to prevent dropout, such as tutoring, socio-vocational guidance, and emotional support. Programs for the prevention of stress, anxiety, and depression, with a focus on resilience and emotional well-being.

<bold>Financial support:</bold> offering scholarship programs, subsidies, and student work opportunities to reduce dropout due to economic reasons. Providing healthy, low-cost food options and access to affordable university housing. <bold>Fostering entrepreneurship:</bold> promoting the creation of startup incubators, innovation training, and access to networking. Training programs in technological tools, cybersecurity, and data management to adapt to the digital world. <bold>Community participation:</bold> involving families, teachers, civil society organizations (CSOs), and governments to strengthen commitment to education. <bold>Volunteering and social commitment:</bold> connecting students with the community, promoting social responsibility and active citizenship.

Quality educational persistence is essential to reduce social inequality and ensure the development of citizens prepared to contribute to the progress of society. Guaranteeing educational persistence with quality standards is a challenge. In this context, the work of civil society organizations becomes a key factor in strengthening access, equity, and excellence. This implies that universities, both public and private, must open themselves to establishing partnerships focused on dropout prevention and the improvement of persistence. They must be willing to mobilize their well-being systems to focus on the individual experiencing the greatest academic, socio-emotional, psychosocial, and economic vulnerability, in a systemic and articulated manner. Action with CSOs reinforces the right to education, contributes to quality persistence, and helps prepare students to face the challenges of the future.

desafios-fatv-en-tecnologia.alt: Article on the challenges of the Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción regarding technology
desafios-fatv-en-tecnologia.title: WHAT CHALLENGES DOES THE FATV FACE REGARDING TECHNOLOGY?

detail.title: WHAT CHALLENGES DOES THE FATV FACE REGARDING TECHNOLOGY?
content: The Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción is committed to leading these efforts, ensuring that technology and data become allies in building a more inclusive and equitable educational future.

Technology and data are essential to guarantee quality and equity in education, but they also present challenges for the Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción. For this year, one of the greatest challenges is ensuring that technology acts as a bridge toward quality education, especially when implementing solutions adapted to the context of each community through innovative and collaborative approaches.

The management and analysis of educational, socioeconomic, and cultural data are crucial for designing personalized strategies. However, consolidating this information is complex due to access restrictions and quality issues, such as incomplete or outdated data. To overcome these obstacles, it is fundamental to establish clear standards and implement data validation and cleaning processes.

Continuous learning in emerging technologies, such as artificial intelligence and predictive analytics, offers opportunities to identify needs and personalize learning processes. Furthermore, web development facilitates access to educational resources, interaction between students and teachers, and the monitoring of academic performance. Designing accessible, user-centered platforms maximizes their impact.

Collaboration and innovation are key to addressing these challenges. By working together with internal departments, external partners, and educational communities, <bold>the Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción is committed to leading these efforts, ensuring that technology and data become allies in building a more inclusive and equitable educational future.</bold>

tikso-2025.alt: Participation of the Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción in Tikso 2025, a space for learning.
tikso-2025.title: FATV PARTICIPATION IN TIKSO 2025

detail.title: FATV'S PARTICIPATION IN TIKSO 2025
content: Thanks to MAKAIA for making such an inspiring Tikso 2025 possible. It was two days filled with learning, networking, and new perspectives to strengthen the impact of social organizations.

We took away valuable insights on sustainability strategies, innovation, and resource mobilization, as well as experiences that prove collaboration is key to transforming realities. We also participated in the business matchmaking session, a space that allowed us to present the work of the Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción, share our mission, and converse with potential partners with whom we hope to build long-lasting impact.

This gathering reminded us of the importance of continuing to innovate, learning from other organizations, and strengthening collaborative networks to respond to social challenges with more sustainable and far-reaching solutions. Thank you to everyone who was part of this experience! We are already looking forward to Tikso 2026.

tejiendo-redes.alt: Our deputy director Víctor Manuel Valencia Martínez attends the international mission on social innovation and sustainability promoted by the Federación Antioqueña de ONG
tejiendo-redes.title: WE CONTINUE WEAVING NETWORKS FOR SOCIAL TRANSFORMATION

detail.title: WE CONTINUE WEAVING NETWORKS FOR SOCIAL TRANSFORMATION
content: The Fundación Antivirus para la Deserción continues to expand its horizons. Our deputy director, <bold>Víctor Manuel Valencia Martínez,</bold> is representing us in the international mission on social innovation and sustainability promoted by the <bold>Federación Antioqueña de ONG.</bold> This is a space for learning, connection, and cooperation that allows us not only to learn but also to share our socio-pedagogical support model with partners from other countries.
This experience encourages us to continue creating global networks of knowledge and action, reaffirming that social and educational challenges are best faced when tackled together.

detail.title: COLLOQUIUM AT ENVIADO UNIVERSITY
content: The Antivirus for Desertion Foundation was invited to the 1st Meeting of Industrial and Related Engineering Programs: Challenges and trends.

The director of our Foundation, Luis Fernando Sánchez, was on October 14 participating in this teacher training event held by the REDIN and ACOFI Network, where they talked about teaching practice as a factor associated with permanence or dropout and about early warnings to address the mental health of engineers in training.

detail.title: WE RECEIVED AN HONORABLE MENTION
content: The Germán Saldarriaga del Valle 2022 award, given each year by the Rotary Club of Medellín, was in charge of rewarding non-profit institutions whose contribution had provided a solution to the difficulties and educational gaps of the community in times of the Covid-19 pandemic, adapting and innovating within this field. Of the 102 proposals presented from 14 departments of the country, five were the winners of the award and three received an honorable mention, among them the Antivirus for Desertion Foundation.

congreso-latinoamericano.alt: Participation in the XI Latin American congress on dropout in higher education
congreso-latinoamericano.title: PARTICIPATION IN THE XI LATIN AMERICAN CONGRESS ON DROPOUT IN HIGHER EDUCATION

detail.title: PARTICIPATION IN THE XI LATIN AMERICAN CONGRESS ON DROPOUT IN HIGHER EDUCATION
content: The article "Antivirus for Desertion Foundation: From the psychopedagogical to the sociopedagogical" written and presented by collaborators Víctor Valencia, Jhisel Holguín and Gabriel Castellanos was the successful bet they developed for the presentation at the XI Latin American Congress on Dropout in Higher Education, held in Brasília, on November 16, 2022. This represented an achievement, since it allowed to chart new horizons, present the intervention model currently developed in the projects and share experiences with attendees from several countries in Latin America.

whyChooseUs.title: WHY CHOOSE US TO TRANSFORM
whyChooseUs.description: We implement proven strategies that generate measurable results in student retention and graduation.

cards.methodology_description: Our strategies are backed by years of experience and measurable results in educational institutions nationwide.
cards.focus_description: We work with clear KPIs (Key Performance Indicators): reduced dropout rates, increased graduation rates, and improved student satisfaction.
cards.accompaniment_description: We don't just design strategies; we implement them alongside your team, ensuring knowledge transfer.
cards.experience_description: We have worked with universities, bootcamps, schools, and scholarship programs, understanding the particularities of each context.
cards.solutions_description: Every institution is unique. We design strategies tailored to your reality, organizational culture, and specific goals.
cards.innovation_description: We incorporate the latest trends in education and technology to keep your programs at the forefront.

testimonials_content.description: The support and the people who provide it are very important because you feel confident and feel like you're learning something... The fact that they come with questions that don't force you but encourage you to give an answer has made me better prepared for my future or at least have a clearer plan for what I'm doing today for my future.
testimonials_content.author: - Estiwar Espinal

testimonials_content.description: We've learned to know ourselves quite well. It has been a personal growth; I've learned to love myself, to know myself more deeply, to look at what I want for my life in a few years, and to know that I have made great progress. I have to be consistent with what I want to achieve and have fixed goals. In the future, I imagine myself as a great professional, having my own company or brand, or being independent.
testimonials_content.author: - Salomé Arango

testimonials_content.description: Honestly, before Antivirus arrived, I was very insecure about what I was going to study or what I was going to do with my life because nobody knows what will happen in the future because it's uncertain. But Antivirus came and little by little has been able to give us security, it has taught us that everything in life has a solution and our problems can become strengths for ourselves.
testimonials_content.author: - Samuel Durango Baena

testimonials_content.description: Antivirus has done a wonderful job. Thanks to Antivirus, I've tried to let go of that fear of the future, it has calmed things down a lot. As 11th-grade students, we carry several responsibilities and we worry a lot about what will happen after school. What will I do? And Antivirus has helped us get to know ourselves a little more and know what I can dedicate myself to or which path to take.
testimonials_content.author: - Antonia Aristizábal

testimonials_content.description: The support seemed very pleasant to me; my biggest learning was in decision-making, understanding my emotions better, and thinking before acting. You helped me become more confident in the decisions I want to make in my life. What I liked the most were the talks, the methodology, and the topics; that enlightened us.
testimonials_content.author: - Matías González
